records.ety: [L. possessus, p. p. of possidere to have, possess, from an inseparable prep. (cf. Position) + sedere to sit. See Sit.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Possessed (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Possessing.]
usage: -- Possess, Have. Have is the more general word. To possess denotes to have as a property. It usually implies more permanence or definiteness of control or ownership than is involved in having. A man does not possess his wife and children: they are (so to speak) part of himself. For the same reason, we have the faculties of reason, understanding, will, sound judgment, etc.: they are exercises of the mind, not possessions.

defs: To occupy in person; to hold or actually have in one's own keeping; to have and to hold.
quotes: Houses and fields and vineyards shall be possessed again in this land.
Yet beauty, though injurious, hath strange power, After offense returning, to regain Love once possessed.

defs: To have the legal title to; to have a just right to; to be master of; to own; to have; as, to possess property, an estate, a book.
quotes: I am yours, and all that I possess.

defs: To obtain occupation or possession of; to accomplish; to gain; to seize.
quotes: How . . . to possess the purpose they desired.

defs: To enter into and influence; to control the will of; to fill; to affect; -- said especially of evil spirits, passions, etc.
quotes: Those which were possessed with devils.
For ten inspired, ten thousand are possessed.

defs: To put in possession; to make the owner or holder of property, power, knowledge, etc.; to acquaint; to inform; -- followed by of or with before the thing possessed, and now commonly used reflexively.
quotes: I have possessed your grace of what I purpose.
Record a gift . . . of all he dies possessed Unto his son.
We possessed our selves of the kingdom of Naples.
To possess our minds with an habitual good intention.
